name: Bird ID: Europe
description: Identify 61 of Europe's best-known garden, park, farmland, and waterside birds from photos. Details give the scientific name, key field marks, and how to separate the classic confusion pairs.

fields.extra: Erithacus rubecula. Round little bird with an orange-red face and breast, olive-brown back, and big dark eye. Famously tame in gardens; sings year-round, even on winter nights under streetlights.
Cyanistes caeruleus. Tiny acrobat with a sky-blue cap, white face crossed by a black eye-line, yellow breast, and blue wings. Great tit is larger with a black head and black belly stripe.
Parus major. Largest European tit: glossy black head with white cheeks and a black stripe running down the yellow belly (broad in males). Two-note 'tea-cher, tea-cher' song.
Turdus merula. Male jet-black with a yellow-orange bill and eye-ring; female dark brown. Hops across lawns; one of Europe's finest songsters at dusk.
Pica pica. Pied corvid with an iridescent blue-green sheen and a very long graduated tail; struts and hops boldly. Loud machine-gun chatter.
Garrulus glandarius. Pinkish-buff corvid with an electric-blue barred wing patch, black mustache, and white rump obvious in flight. Shy; screeches harshly and buries thousands of acorns.
Columba palumbus. Big bulky pigeon with a white neck patch, pinkish breast, and white wing crescents in flight. Clatters loudly out of trees; five-note cooing song.
Streptopelia decaocto. Pale sandy-gray dove with a thin black half-collar on the nape. Colonized nearly all of Europe from the Balkans in the 20th century; monotonous three-note 'coo-COO-cuk'.

Passer domesticus. Male has a gray crown, black bib, and chestnut sides of the head; female plain gray-brown. Chirps around buildings everywhere; tree sparrow instead has an all-brown crown and a black cheek spot.
Passer montanus. Like a neat small house sparrow but with a chocolate-brown crown and a black spot on each white cheek; sexes alike. More rural than the house sparrow.
Sturnus vulgaris. Glossy purple-green with a yellow bill in summer, white-spangled in winter; short tail, triangular wings. Gathers in vast swirling murmurations; a gifted mimic.
Fringilla coelebs. Male has a blue-gray crown, pink-rust face and breast, and double white wing bars; female drab but shows the same wing bars. One of Europe's most numerous birds.
Carduelis carduelis. Crimson face bordered black-and-white, with a broad golden-yellow wing bar; sexes alike. Tinkling flight calls; flocks feed on thistles and teasels.
Chloris chloris. Stocky olive-green finch with yellow flashes along the wing edge and tail sides and a heavy pale bill. Wheezy drawn-out 'dzweee' song note.
Pyrrhula pyrrhula. Male has a rose-pink breast, black cap, gray back, and white rump; female pinkish-gray. Quiet and shy; soft piping call from hedgerows.
Spinus spinus. Small streaky yellow-green finch; male has a black cap and chin with yellow wing bars and rump. Acrobatic in alders and birches, often hanging upside down.
Coccothraustes coccothraustes. Bull-necked orange-brown finch with a massive steel-blue bill that cracks cherry stones; white wing bar and tail tip. Elusive in treetops.
Periparus ater. Small tit with a black cap and bib and a distinctive white patch on the nape; buff-gray body without yellow. Prefers conifers.
Aegithalos caudatus. Tiny pink-black-and-white puffball whose tail is longer than its body. Roams hedgerows in chattering family flocks; weaves a domed lichen-and-cobweb nest.
Lophophanes cristatus. The only small European songbird with a pointed black-and-white speckled crest; black bridle across the face. Conifer forests; in Britain only Scots pinewoods of Scotland.
Troglodytes troglodytes. Minute round brown bird with a barred, cocked tail and thin bill; creeps mouse-like through undergrowth, then explodes into a huge trilling song.
Prunella modularis. Unobtrusive shuffler under hedges: streaky brown back with a lead-gray head and breast and a thin insect-eater's bill - not a true sparrow, despite the alias 'hedge sparrow'.
Regulus regulus. Europe's smallest bird (about 5 g): olive-green with a black-bordered yellow-orange crown stripe. Flits restlessly through conifers with thin high calls.
Sitta europaea. Blue-gray above, buff-orange below, with a black bandit stripe through the eye; the only European bird that climbs headfirst down trunks. Plasters mud around its nest hole.

Certhia familiaris. Bark-patterned little bird with a fine curved bill and stiff tail; spirals UP a trunk like a mouse, then flies to the base of the next tree. Nuthatches go down; treecreepers only up.
Dendrocopos major. Black and white with big white shoulder patches and scarlet under the tail; male has a red nape patch. The bird behind most drumming heard in European woods.
Picus viridis. Large green woodpecker with a red crown and black face; yellow rump flashes in bounding flight. Feeds on ground ants; loud laughing 'yaffle' call, rarely drums.
Turdus philomelos. Warm brown above with neat arrow-shaped spots on a cream breast. Repeats each song phrase 2-4 times; smashes snails on stone 'anvils'. Mistle thrush is grayer with round spots.

Turdus viscivorus. Big, pot-bellied, gray-brown thrush with round dark spots and white tail corners; stands upright on open ground. Sings far-carrying phrases from treetops in storms. Song thrush is warmer brown with arrowhead spots.
Turdus pilaris. Handsome winter thrush with a blue-gray head and rump, chestnut back, and speckled ochre breast. Chattering 'chack-chack' flocks strip berry hedges.
Turdus iliacus. Small dark winter thrush with a bold cream eyebrow and rusty-red flanks and underwings. Migrates at night; thin 'tseep' calls overhead in autumn.
Sylvia atricapilla. Gray warbler with a neat cap: glossy black in the male, rust-brown in the female. Rich fluty warble earned it the name 'northern nightingale'.
Saxicola rubicola. Robin-sized bird of gorse and heath; male has a black head, white half-collar, and orange breast. Perches atop bushes flicking wings; call like two stones knocked together.
Emberiza citrinella. Farmland bunting; male has a lemon-yellow head and belly with a chestnut rump. Song traditionally rendered 'a little bit of bread and no cheese'.
Motacilla alba. Slim gray-black-and-white bird that runs across pavement and rooftops constantly bobbing its long tail. The British subspecies, the pied wagtail, is blacker-backed.
Hirundo rustica. Steel-blue above with a dark red throat, pale belly, and long deeply forked tail streamers. Builds open mud cups on beams; its arrival marks spring across Europe.
Apus apus. All-dark scythe-winged screamer that never perches: it feeds, sleeps, and mates on the wing, landing only at the nest. Screaming parties race around rooftops on summer evenings.
Delichon urbicum. Blue-black above with a clean white belly and a bold white rump, tail only shallowly forked. Builds closed mud nests under eaves. Swallows have red throats and long streamers.
Alcedo atthis. Jewel of slow rivers: electric-blue back, orange underparts, dagger bill. Usually seen as a blue streak flying low over water with a shrill whistle; dives for minnows from perches.
Upupa epops. Unmistakable: pinkish-cinnamon with zebra-striped wings, a long curved bill, and a huge fan crest. Soft far-carrying 'oop-oop-oop' gave it its name.
Merops apiaster. Rainbow bird of southern Europe: chestnut crown, golden back, turquoise belly, yellow throat, black mask. Catches bees and dragonflies in graceful sallies; nests in sandbank tunnels.
Oriolus oriolus. Male brilliant yellow with black wings; female greenish. Despite the color, maddeningly hard to see in poplar canopies - find it by the fluty 'wee-la-weeo' whistle.
Cuculus canorus. Gray hawk-like profile with a barred breast and pointed wings; the two-note 'cuck-oo' announces spring. Lays its eggs in other birds' nests, one host species per female line.
Corvus corone. All-black crow with a neat feathered bill base, usually alone or in pairs. Rook has a bare gray-white bill base and baggy 'trousers' and feeds in flocks.
Corvus frugilegus. Glossy black corvid with a bare grayish-white face and bill base and a peaked crown; nests in noisy treetop rookeries and feeds in flocks on fields.
Coloeus monedula. Small dapper crow with a gray nape and hood and striking pale silver eyes. Nests in chimneys and church towers; sharp 'jack!' call.
Phasianus colchicus. Male copper-gold with a bottle-green head, red face wattles, and a very long barred tail; female mottled buff. Introduced from Asia; explodes noisily from field edges.
Ardea cinerea. Tall gray heron with a white head and neck, black eyebrow-plume, and dagger bill. Stands statue-still at pond edges; flies with slow beats, neck tucked back.
Ciconia ciconia. Huge white bird with black flight feathers and a long red bill and legs; flies with neck outstretched. Nests on rooftops and poles; the baby-delivering stork of legend.
Cygnus olor. Enormous white swan with an orange bill topped by a black knob; swims with neck in an S curve and often with wings arched. Wings throb audibly in flight.
Podiceps cristatus. Elegant diving waterbird with a black double crest and chestnut-and-black head frills in summer. Pairs perform a famous mirrored 'weed dance' courtship.
Gallinula chloropus. Blackish pond bird with a RED bill and forehead shield tipped yellow, a white flank line, and flicking white undertail. The coot's shield is white.
Fulica atra. Sooty-black waterbird with a WHITE bill and frontal shield and lobed toes; dives constantly and runs across the water to fight rivals. The moorhen's shield is red.

Chroicocephalus ridibundus. Small gull with a chocolate-brown (not black) hood in summer, reduced to an ear spot in winter; red bill and legs, white leading edge on the wing. Europe's everyday park gull.
Vanellus vanellus. Farmland plover with a wispy upswept crest, green-glossed black back, and broad rounded wings; tumbling display flight with a 'pee-wit' cry.
Buteo buteo. Europe's default soaring raptor: compact, broad-winged, with a short rounded tail and mewing cat-like call; plumage varies from dark brown to nearly white.
Milvus milvus. Rusty-red raptor with long angled wings, white underwing flashes, and a deeply forked tail it twists like a rudder. A conservation comeback story in Britain.
Falco tinnunculus. The falcon that hangs motionless over road verges, hovering into the wind with fanned tail; male has a gray head and tail with a chestnut back.

Accipiter nisus. Short-winged, long-tailed ambush hawk that dashes along hedges into garden feeders; male slate-gray above with rusty barring, female browner and much larger. Kestrels hover; sparrowhawks never do.
Strix aluco. Chunky round-headed brown owl with big black eyes; the classic 'twit-twoo' is a duet - her 'ke-wick', his wavering hoot. Strictly nocturnal woodland resident.
Tyto alba. Ghostly pale owl with a heart-shaped white face and golden-buff upperparts; quarters fields silently at dusk and shrieks rather than hoots.
